title: Birdhouse Bath Shelf with Towel Bar
summary: A birdhouse-roofline shelf with a built-in wooden towel bar and a drop shelf for folded towels.
description:
The two side pieces are the one place this build asks for real care: they're cut as true mirror images, both beveled 30° so the roofline peaks upward, and both need matching holes drilled for the towel bar to pass through. Drill the towel-bar holes before you cut the final profile — corner-to-corner on a scrap test piece first, since lining up two holes on two separate boards so a single rod slides through both cleanly is fussier than it looks on paper.

Everything after that is straightforward glue-and-nail assembly: shelf between the sides, a mitered "roof" cap on top, a plywood or beadboard back, and a drop-ledge underneath for a second, lower surface. The optional double robe hooks are worth the small extra step if your bathroom wall space is tight — the larger single hooks shown as an alternative need more clearance than most powder rooms have.

tools.note: Cuts the two side profiles — practice the mirrored bevel-and-hole layout on scrap first.
For the towel-bar holes through both sides.

materials.name: Wooden closet rod, 1" dia
materials.note: The towel bar — cut to fit between the sides.

materials.name: Double robe hooks
materials.note: Optional. Fit tighter wall spaces than the larger single hooks.

cutList.note: Longest-point measurement; top end beveled 30° off square.
Top end beveled 30° off square, longest-point measurement — cut as true mirror images with matching towel-bar holes.
Top end angled 30° off square, longest-point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the two sides as mirror images
steps.body: Cut the two side pieces with a jigsaw, keeping in mind the top bevels must angle upward on BOTH sides — that means the two sides are cut as true mirror images of each other, not identical copies. Drill the towel-bar opening in all four corners with a drill bit, then connect the dots with the jigsaw; this is trickier than it sounds, so practice on a scrap piece first. Sand both jigsaw-cut edges now so they match and are smooth.

steps.title: Attach the shelf
steps.body: With both sides oriented so their bevels point upward, glue and nail the shelf between them.

steps.title: Attach the roof cap
steps.body: Mark the roof cap pieces on both their top and underside so you know exactly where and how to line them up. If you've already cut the back plywood, lay it on top of the shelf assembly and use it as an alignment pattern by matching edges. Attach the roof cap with nails and glue, and double-check its angle matches the roof trim you'll add in a later step.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Attach the back panel with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue.

steps.title: Add the roof trim
steps.body: Glue and nail the roof trim pieces on, matching the angle set by the roof cap.

steps.title: Add the drop ledge
steps.body: Glue and nail the drop ledge on below the main shelf, overhanging the ends by 1/2".

steps.title: Install the towel bar
steps.body: Thread the closet-rod towel bar through the holes in the two sides and glue it in place.

steps.title: Add hooks (optional)
steps.body: If you'd like hanging hooks, install double robe hooks below the shelf — they fit tighter wall spaces than the larger single hooks shown as an alternative.

images.alt: Wall-mounted bathroom shelf with a birdhouse-style peaked roof and a built-in wooden towel bar
